```{r model-trajectory-pr, fig.width=18, fig.height=9, out.width="100%", dpi=300, warning=FALSE, message=FALSE}
             aes(yintercept = mean_prauc, color = model_label, linetype = "Overall Average"), 
             linewidth = 1.2, alpha = 0.7) +
  
  geom_line(data = plot_data, aes(x = year, y = prauc, color = model_label), linewidth = 4.5, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(data = plot_data, aes(x = year, y = prauc, color = model_label, linetype = "Yearly Trend"), linewidth = 1.8) +
  
  geom_point(data = plot_data, aes(x = year, y = prauc, fill = model_label), size = 5, shape = 21, stroke = 1.5, color = "white") +
  
  geom_text(data = end_points, 
            aes(label = label_text, x = year + 0.3, y = prauc, color = model_label),
            hjust = 0, family = "montserrat", fontface = "bold", size = 5, lineheight = 0.9) +
  
  scale_color_manual(values = c("GLM (Baseline)" = "#95A5A6", "XGBoost (GPU)" = "#D35400", "Convolutional Neural Network" = "#2980B9")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("GLM (Baseline)" = "#95A5A6", "XGBoost (GPU)" = "#D35400", "Convolutional Neural Network" = "#2980B9")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(name = NULL, values = c("Yearly Trend" = "solid", "Overall Average" = "dashed")) +
  
  scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 0.35), breaks = seq(0, 0.35, 0.05), expand = c(0, 0.02)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = unique(plot_data$year), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.22))) +
  
  labs(
    title = "Predictive Trajectory: Expanding Window Validation",
    subtitle = "Comparing advanced architectures (<b style='color:#D35400;'>XGBoost</b> and <b style='color:#2980B9;'>Neural Networks</b>) against a <b style='color:#95A5A6;'>GLM baseline</b>.",
    caption = "Validation Strategy: Expanding Window | Metric: Precision-Recall Area Under the Curve (PR AUC)",
    x = NULL, 
    y = NULL
  ) +
  
  # Theme
  theme_void(base_family = "montserrat") +
  theme(
    plot.margin = margin(40, 40, 40, 40),
    
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", size = 28, color = "#111111", margin = margin(b = 10)),
    plot.subtitle = element_markdown(size = 20, color = "#555555", margin = margin(b = 15)),
    plot.caption = element_text(size = 13, color = "#888888", hjust = 0, margin = margin(t = 30)),
    
    axis.text.x = element_text(color = "#555555", size = 15, margin = margin(t = 15)),
    axis.text.y = element_text(color = "#555555", size = 15, margin = margin(r = 10)),
    
    legend.position = "top",
    legend.justification = "right",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 16, color = "#333333", face = "bold"),
    legend.key.width = unit(4, "cm"),
    legend.margin = margin(b = 20)
  ) +
  guides(color = "none", fill = "none") 

p_prauc
```

While the absolute values for the PR-AUC are much lower, due to the class imbalance and the inherent variability of the minority class, the trends are similar to the AUC graph, and the models demonstrate strong performance overall. The CNN model performs the best, achieving a PR-AUC of **0.280**, while the XGBoost model has an average PR-AUC of **0.139**. Lagging behind is the logistic regression model, which has an average PR-AUC of 0.10. 

This gives a far better idea of the **true performance** of the model. Given that the rate of notable fires is slightly under 1%, these PR-AUC scores indicate a far higher ability to predict the **minority class**, demonstrating the capability of the model. Most notable is the **extremely high scores of the CNN model**. This indicates that its grid capture of spatial relationships is a key predictor in performance, which allowed it to separate true underlying relationships instead of relying on predicting the majority class.

<h1 class="custom-section">

SECTION V: Summary

</h1>

In conclusion, this project successfully developed a **robust, spatiotemporal** predictive model capable of proactively assessing wildfire risk across the contiguous United States. By standardizing over 1.88 million records into a 0.5 degree by 0.5 degree grid,integrating high resolution weather and environmental data which covered all areas of covariates related to wildfires, creating a binary outcome result for each month, and introducing lagged variables of key predictors, we created a final model with over 40 features, which includes temporal, spatial, and historical dynamics integral to wildfire prediction.

During our exploratory data analysis, we revealed several key insights integral to future modeling. The class imbalance and high degree of clustering within the data informed key decisions regarding the way incorrect predictions of the minority class should be considered, and the **strong multicollinearity** concerns informed the decision to focus on models with in-built regularization techniques.

To guard against leakage, ensure temporal integrity, and create a robust model, a time-aware validation strategy was used, in which models at year **t** were trained on data from years **1992 to t-1**, and tested on year **t**. This process was repeated across many folds, from 1995 to 2015, the end of the wildfire dataset. More advanced machine learning techniques delivered more impressive results:

-   **Convolutional Neural Network** emerged as the superior model, achieving an outstanding average AUC of **0.917** and a PR-AUC of **0.280**. This indicates that patterns with surrounding land areas is important for prediction of wildfires, and also indicates the strength of the CNN architecture in capturing complex, non-linear relationships in data.
-   **XGBoost** model delivered strong performance, with an AUC of **0.901** and a PR-AUC of **0.139**. Its tree-based ensemble architecture demonstrated exceptional performance in capturing the complex, non-linear relationships between the predictors and final outcome variable.
-   The **Logistic Regression (GLM)** baseline yielded a respectable average AUC of **0.845**, but its simplicity ultimately limited it from uncovering more complex interactions in the data. The PR-AUC was **0.1**, which is still significantly stronger than random guessing.

[Ultimately, this project highlights the power of modern machine learning and its applicability to a critical real-world problem. The final Convolutional Neural Network model provides an accurate predictive tool that can analyze local and historical climate profiles to deliver a quantitative assessment of fire risk, providing a powerful resource for fire management and resource allocation strategies.]{style="color: blue;"}}

<h1 class="custom-section">

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

</h1>

The following resources were used for data:

<h2 class="custom-subsection">

PRISM Climate Group (2026). PRISM Time Series Spatial Climate Dataset for the Conterminous United States. Oregon State University. Available at: <https://prism.oregonstate.edu>.

Copernicus Climate Change Service (C3S) (2023): ERA5 [Hourly/Monthly] data on single levels from 1940 to present. Copernicus Climate Data Store (CDS). DOI: 10.24381/cds.adbb2d47.

Tatman, Rachael. (2018). 1.88 Million US Wildfires. Version 2. Kaggle. Available at: <https://www.kaggle.com/rtatman/188-million-us-wildfires>.

</h2>
